primary_diagnosis.name: Lichen planus (cutaneous and oral), acute flare

pathophysiology_summary: Lichen planus is a T-cell mediated inflammatory disease attacking basal keratinocytes, producing the classic 'six Ps' — pruritic, purple (violaceous), planar (flat-topped), polygonal papules/plaques — typically on flexor wrists/ankles, with Wickham striae (fine white reticulate surface network), Koebnerisation (new lesions along scratch lines) and characteristic lacy white oral (buccal) plaques. The itch is intense and 'deep', out of proportion to the rash. Discriminators: violaceous colour, flexural distribution, Wickham striae and absence of thick silvery scale distinguish it from psoriasis; polygonal planar papules with finger-web sparing and no burrows argue against scabies; the absence of a herald patch and acral (not truncal) distribution argue against pityriasis rosea. Must-not-miss considerations: a lichenoid drug eruption/severe cutaneous adverse reaction (screen for new drugs, widespread blistering, skin peeling — absent here) and erosive oral LP (painful ulceration threatening intake/premalignant risk). LP is also associated with hepatitis C, so serology is checked. Management is potent topical corticosteroids, itch/sleep support, and a short oral corticosteroid course for a severe flare, with baseline bloods and dermatology follow-up for chronic disease.

differential.evidence_basis: Six Ps + Wickham striae + oral lacy plaques + Koebner.
Screen for a new drug trigger, widespread blistering or skin peeling.
Extensor, silvery-scaled, not violaceous with Wickham striae.
Intense itch but favours finger-webs with burrows, not polygonal planar papules.
Truncal with a herald patch, not intensely violaceous acral papules.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose LP from the six Ps/Wickham striae/oral plaques, screen for drug eruption/SCAR, and check HCV serology.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic LP; no new drug/blistering/peeling; oral lacy (non-erosive) plaques.
No new drug, blistering or skin peeling — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: potent topical ± short oral corticosteroid, itch/sleep support, HCV serology, dermatology follow-up; SCAR/erosive-oral safety-net.

red_flags.description: Severe erosive oral lichen planus (painful open ulcers preventing eating/drinking)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Erosive oral LP can impair intake and carries a small premalignant risk — needs specialist care.

red_flags.description: Severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction / widespread blistering (SJS/TEN-type)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A severe blistering drug reaction is life-threatening.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening for a lichenoid drug eruption or severe cutaneous adverse reaction.
Relying on OTC creams for a deep immune-mediated itch that needs potent topical ± oral steroids.
Not checking hepatitis C serology or documenting/monitoring oral LP.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe potent topical ± short oral corticosteroid and itch/sleep support, arrange baseline bloods incl. HCV serology and dermatology follow-up; urgent review/ED for erosive oral disease preventing intake or widespread blistering.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a drug eruption/SCAR, or under-treating with OTC creams.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Lichen planus: potent topical corticosteroids first-line; short oral corticosteroid for severe generalised flares; screen for hepatitis C; refer erosive oral LP for specialist care and surveillance.
